analogy: Think of "Current Electricity" through the chapter's own opening images: 3.1 INTRODUCTION In Chapter 1, all charges whether free or bound, were considered to be at rest. Charges in motion constitute an electric current. Such currents occur naturally in many situations. Lightning is one such phenomenon in which charges flow from the clouds to the... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 3.1 INTRODUCTION, 3.2 ELECTRIC CURRENT, CURRENT, ELECTRICITY. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Current Electricity is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 3.1 INTRODUCTION In Chapter 1, all charges whether free or bound, were considered to be at rest.

The text explains that charges in motion constitute an electric current.

The text explains that such currents occur naturally in many situations.

The text explains that lightning is one such phenomenon in which charges flow from the clouds to the earth through the atmosphere, sometimes with disastrous results.

The text explains that the flow of charges in lightning is not steady, but in our everyday life we see many devices where charges flow in a steady manner, like water flowing smoothly in a river.

The text explains that a torch and a cell-driven clock are examples of such devices.

The text explains that in the present chapter, we shall study some of the basic laws concerning steady electric currents.

The text explains that 3.2 ELECTRIC CURRENT Imagine a small area held normal to the direction of flow of charges.

The text explains that both the positive and the negative charges may flow forward and backward across the area.

The text explains that in a given time interval t, let q+ be the net amount (i.e., forward minus backward) of positive charge that flows in the forward direction across the area.

The text explains that similarly, let q– be the net amount of negative charge flowing across the area in the forward direction.

The text explains that the net amount of charge flowing across the area in the forward direction in the time interval t, then, is q = q+– q–.

The text explains that this is proportional to t for steady current Chapter Three CURRENT ELECTRICITY

The text explains that physics 82 and the quotient q I t = (3.1) is defined to be the current across the area in the forward direction.

The text explains that (If it turn out to be a negative number, it implies a current in the backward direction.) Currents are not always steady and hence more generally, we define the current as follows.

The text explains that let DQ be the net charge flowing across a cross- section of a conductor during the time interval Dt [i.e., between times t and (t + Dt)].

The text explains that an ampere is defined through magnetic effects of currents that we will study in the following chapter.

The text explains that however, the direction of its velocity after the collision is completely random.

The text explains that at a given time, there is no preferential direction for the velocities of the electrons.

The text explains that current Electricity 83 number of electrons travelling in any direction will be equal to the number of electrons travelling in the opposite direction.

The text explains that an electric field will be created and is directed from the positive towards the negative charge.

The text explains that mechanisms, which maintain a steady electric field are cells or batteries that we shall study later in this chapter.

The text explains that using the last equation, Ohm’s law reads I l V I R A ρ = × = (3.10) Current per unit area (taken normal to the current), I/A, is called current density and is denoted by j.

The text explains that the SI units of the current density are A/m2.

The text explains that ohm was led to his law by an analogy between the conduction of heat: the electric field is analogous to the temperature gradient, and the electric current is analogous to the heat flow.

The text explains that the current density, (which we have defined as the current through unit area normal to the current) is also directed along E, and is also a vector j (ººººº j E/E).

The text explains that 3.5 DRIFT OF ELECTRONS AND THE ORIGIN OF RESISTIVITY As remarked before, an electron will suffer collisions with the heavy fixed ions, but after collision, it will emerge with the same speed but in random directions.

The text explains that if we consider all the electrons, their average velocity will be zero since their directions are random.

The text explains that (3.14)] since immediately after any collision, the direction of the velocity of an electron is completely random.

The text explains that a slight drift in a direction opposite the electric field is visible.

The text explains that since each electron carries a charge –e, the total charge transported across this area A to the right in time Dt is –ne A|vd|Dt.

The text explains that e is directed towards the left and hence the total charge transported along E across the area is negative of this.

The text explains that (3.17) 2 E τ ∆ = ∆ e A I t n t m (3.19) By definition I is related to the magnitude |j| of the current density by I = |j|A (3.20) Hence, from Eqs.(3.19) and (3.20), 2 j E τ = ne m (3.21) The vector j is parallel to E and hence we can write Eq.

The text explains that the magnitude of current density in a metal is the magnitude of charge contained in a cylinder of unit area and length vd.

The text explains that the density of copper is 9.0 × 103 kg/m3, and its atomic mass is 63.5 u.

The text explains that (b) Compare the drift speed obtained above with, (i) thermal speeds of copper atoms at ordinary temperatures, (ii) speed of propagation of electric field along the conductor which causes the drift motion.

The text explains that solution (a) The direction of drift velocity of conduction electrons is opposite to the electric field direction, i.e., electrons drift in the direction of increasing potential.

The text explains that the density of conduction electrons, n is equal to the number of atoms per cubic metre (assuming one conduction electron per Cu atom as is reasonable from its valence electron count of one).

The text explains that since 6.0 × 1023 copper atoms have a mass of 63.5 g, 23 6 6.0 10 9.0 10 63.5 n × = × × = 8.5 × 1028 m–3 which gives, 28 –19 –7 1.5 8.5 10 1.6 10 1.0 10 = × × × × × d v = 1.1 × 10–3 m s–1 = 1.1 mm s–1 (b) (i) At a temperature T, the thermal speed* of a copper atom of mass M is obtained from [<(1/2) Mv2 > = (3/2) kBT ] and is thus typically of the order of / B k T M , where kB is the Boltzmann constant.

The text explains that note that the drift speed of electrons is much smaller, about 10–5 times the typical thermal speed at ordinary temperatures.

The text explains that (ii) An electric field travelling along the conductor has a speed of an electromagnetic wave, namely equal to 3.0 × 108 m s–1 (You will learn about this in Chapter 8).

The text explains that (d) When electrons drift in a metal from lower to higher potential, does it mean that all the ‘free’ electrons of the metal are moving in the same direction?

The text explains that 3.6 A heating element using nichrome connected to a 230 V supply draws an initial current of 3.2 A which settles after a few seconds to a steady value of 2.8 A.

The text explains that what is the steady temperature of the heating element if the room temperature is 27.0 °C?

The text explains that temperature coefficient of resistance of nichrome averaged over the temperature range involved is 1.70 × 10–4 °C–1.

The text explains that 3.7 Determine the current in each branch of the network shown in Fig.

The text explains that 3.20: FIGURE 3.20 3.8 A storage battery of emf 8.0 V and internal resistance 0.5 W is being charged by a 120 V dc supply using a series resistor of 15.5 W.

The text explains that what is the terminal voltage of the battery during charging?

The text explains that what is the purpose of having a series resistor in the charging circuit?

The text explains that 3.9 The number density of free electrons in a copper conductor estimated in Example 3.1 is 8.5 × 1028 m–3.

The text explains that how long does an electron take to drift from one end of a wire 3.0 m long to its other end?

The text explains that the area of cross-section of the wire is 2.0 × 10–6 m2 and it is carrying a current of 3.0 A.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3: Current Electricity
